items.meaning: Overall home run opportunity score. Combines batter power, pitcher vulnerability, zone fit, weather, park, trend, and lineup context.
Estimated chance of a home run. This is not a guarantee. It should only rise toward elite levels when many independent factors align.
How many key factors are lining up together. A higher stack means batter, pitcher, weather, park, and form signals agree.
How reliable the model read is based on data completeness. TBD pitchers and missing lineups reduce confidence.
Pitcher Vulnerability Index. Higher means the pitcher profile is more home-run friendly.
Killer HR Read. A combined hitter power indicator using power, blast quality, and recent HR form.
Contact violence score based on barrel rate, max exit velocity, and hard-hit ability.
How well the hitter's damage profile fits the expected attack zones/pitching shape.
Recent home-run/contact momentum indicator using recent trend data.
Amount of batter data behind the profile. Larger samples usually improve reliability.
Isolated Power. Slugging percentage minus batting average. Measures extra-base hit power.
How favorable the batter's profile is against the opposing pitcher. N/A when pitcher is TBD.
Expected weighted on-base average based on contact quality, walks, and strikeouts.
Expected weighted on-base average on contact only. Useful for judging quality of batted-ball contact.
Home runs allowed per nine innings. Higher means more home-run risk.
Fly-ball percentage. More fly balls can increase home-run exposure.
Ground-ball percentage. Higher GB% usually lowers home-run risk.
Barrel rate allowed. Barrels are the most dangerous batted-ball contact type.
Hard-hit rate allowed. Measures how often a pitcher allows contact at 95+ mph.
Expected ERA based on quality of contact, strikeouts, and walks.
Fielding Independent Pitching. Estimates pitcher performance using outcomes most controlled by the pitcher.
Skill-Interactive ERA. Advanced ERA estimator using strikeouts, walks, and batted-ball type.
Estimated environmental help or suppression for fly-ball distance.
Wind blowing toward the outfield. Usually helps home runs.
Wind blowing toward home plate. Usually suppresses home runs.
How friendly the stadium is for home runs compared with league average.
Wind blowing from home plate toward the outfield. Usually helps fly balls carry farther.
Wind blowing from the outfield toward home plate. Usually suppresses fly-ball distance.
Wind blowing across the field, such as left-to-right or right-to-left. It can help or hurt depending on spray direction.
How much the weather environment helps or hurts fly-ball distance.
Warmer air is less dense and can help the ball travel farther. Cold air usually suppresses carry.
Moist air can be slightly less dense than dry air, but humidity also interacts with temperature and park conditions.
A measure of moisture in the air. Higher dew points can indicate heavier, more humid game conditions.
How thick the air is. Lower air density generally helps baseballs travel farther.
Whether a stadium roof is open or closed. Closed roofs reduce or remove wind/weather impact.
How much a ballpark increases or decreases home runs compared with league average.
A park-specific measure focused on home run friendliness.
How favorable the park is to the hitter pull side.
Outfield fence height. Taller walls can turn would-be home runs into doubles or outs.
The direction the ballpark faces. This affects how wind direction maps onto left, center, or right field.
